title: BuilderBasedDeserializer unwrapped update path still uses ignorable-only check
body:
While looking at the `@JsonUnwrapped` / ignored-property handling updated in #5749, I noticed one remaining builder-based path that still uses the older ignorable-only check.

In `BuilderBasedDeserializer.deserializeWithUnwrapped(JsonParser, DeserializationContext, Object, TokenBuffer)`, the unknown-property branch currently checks only `_ignorableProps`:

```java
if ((_ignorableProps != null) && _ignorableProps.contains(propName)) {
    handleIgnoredProperty(p, ctxt, builder, propName);
    continue;
}
```

Nearby builder and bean deserialization paths use:

```java
IgnorePropertiesUtil.shouldIgnore(propName, _ignorableProps, _includableProps)
```

For example, the other `BuilderBasedDeserializer` paths already use `shouldIgnore(...)`, as do the corresponding `BeanDeserializer` unwrapped/update paths.

I could not produce a small user-facing reproducer for this 4-argument overload. In the builder update cases I tried, either the unwrapped handler was not active on that path or another overload handled the input first.

So this may be harmless or effectively unreachable, but the code looks like the remaining outlier from the #5749 changes that updated other paths to use `shouldIgnore(...)`. Should this branch also use `IgnorePropertiesUtil.shouldIgnore(...)`, or is there a reason this overload should only consider `_ignorableProps`?

title: Jackson 3 `MapperBuilder.addModule()` is not recursive wrt declared Module dependencies
body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

In Jackson 2, modules could form hierarchies, where registerModule() would be called recursively making sure all dependencies are registered.

Jackson 3's implementation of addModule only goes over the passed module's dependencies and not the whole tree, which makes forming such hierarchies impossible. 

### Version Information

3.1.2

### Reproduction

```scala
import tools.jackson.databind.json.JsonMapper
import tools.jackson.databind.module.SimpleModule

import java.util

object Test {
  object ModuleA extends SimpleModule
  object ModuleB extends SimpleModule {
    override def getDependencies = util.Arrays.asList(ModuleA)
  }
  object ModuleC extends SimpleModule {
    override def getDependencies = util.Arrays.asList(ModuleB)
  }

  def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = {

    val mapper = JsonMapper.builder().addModules(ModuleC).build()
    println(s"ModuleA: ${mapper.registeredModules().contains(ModuleA)}")
    println(s"ModuleB: ${mapper.registeredModules().contains(ModuleB)}")
    println(s"ModuleC: ${mapper.registeredModules().contains(ModuleC)}")
  }
}
```

### Expected behavior

Either a correct registration of all modules or some form of warning or error about the behaviour change.

### Additional context

Encountered while migrating from Jackson 2 to Jackson 3.

title: Forward Object Id references inside Collection properties leak Builder instance (follow-up to #1496)
body:
Follow-up to #1496.

#1496 enabled Object Ids with Builder-based deserialization for the common cases and added clear error reporting (`InvalidDefinitionException`) for forward Object Id references bound to scalar properties, where the reference would otherwise be silently resolved against the discarded `Builder` instance.

One case remains broken: **forward Object Id references that appear as items of a `Collection`-typed property**. The collection-item path does not go through `ObjectIdReferenceProperty.deserializeSetAndReturn`, so `DeserializationContext.addPendingForwardRef` is never called and the guard in `BuilderBasedDeserializer.finishBuild` (`hasPendingForwardRefsFor`) does not trip. As a result:

- No error is reported.
- The unresolved slot ends up holding the `Builder` instance instead of the built target object after resolution.

### Reproduction

See `src/test/java/tools/jackson/databind/tofix/ObjectIdWithBuilder1496Test.java` (`forwardReferenceInCollection`, currently `@JacksonTestFailureExpected`):

```json
{"entities":[
  {"id":1,"refs":[2]},
  {"id":2,"ref":1,"refs":[]}
]}
```

`Entity` uses `@JsonIdentityInfo` + `@JsonDeserialize(builder = EntityBuilder.class)`. After deserialization, `entities[0].refs[0]` is an `EntityBuilder` rather than the built `Entity` with id 2.

```
expected: Entity@... but was: EntityBuilder@...
```

### Suggested fix direction

Two options, both requiring changes beyond the scope of #1496:

1. Plumb collection-item forward references through `addPendingForwardRef` so the existing `BuilderBasedDeserializer.finishBuild` guard catches them and emits the same `InvalidDefinitionException` ("Cannot resolve forward Object Id references for Builder-based type …").
2. Actually support the case: re-bind collection-item references from the builder to the built object when `updateObjectId` is called in `finishBuild`. This would likely require the collection `Referring` implementation to late-bind via the `ReadableObjectId` entry after `updateObjectId` rewrites the bound item.

Option 1 matches the existing fail-fast contract for the scalar case and is the minimum acceptable fix. Option 2 is the full solution.

### Version

3.x (observed on current `3.x` branch after #1496 / commit `476f3d9f5`).

title: Excessive `TokenBuffer` allocations when deserializing records

labels.name: performance
labels.description: Issue related to performance problems or enhancements

body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

When deserializing objects whose construction goes through a property-based creator – which includes every Java record, since records always use the canonical constructor – `BeanDeserializer._deserializeUsingPropertyBased` allocates a fresh `TokenBuffer` on every call. Each `TokenBuffer` allocates at least one `Segment` (an `Object[]` plus a token-type array). For workloads that stream many small records, this shows up prominently in allocation profiles.

Code path:

```
  BeanDeserializer.deserialize
    → BeanDeserializerBase.deserializeFromObjectUsingNonDefault
      → BeanDeserializer._deserializeUsingPropertyBased
        → DeserializationContext.bufferForInputBuffering
          → TokenBuffer.forBuffering   // fresh allocation per object
```

Format-independent — reproduces with JSON, CSV, or any other backend. Since records are now the idiomatic way to model immutable DTOs in Java and unconditionally force this path, users migrating DTOs from classes-with-setters to records see a regression in allocation rate with no change in code shape.

The buffer's lifetime is bounded to _deserializeUsingPropertyBased, so reusing it (with `RecyclerPool`?) may be feasible.


### Version Information

3.1.1

### Reproduction

_No response_

### Expected behavior

_No response_

_No response_

title: Add new extension point for `ObjectMapper`, `ObjectWriter`: handler `GeneratorInitializer` called immediately after `JsonGenerator` created, before used
body:
So there is need to allow registering something like:

```
public interface GeneratorInitializer {
   public void initialize(SerializationConfig cfg, JsonGenerator g) throws JacksonException
}
```

that can be configured on `SerializationConfig` via `ObjectMapperBuilder` or using new `ObjectWriter.with(GeneratorInitializer initializer)` method.

Method "initialize()" will need to be called immediately after construction of `JsonGenerator` and before it is used (by various `ObjectMapper` and `ObjectWriter` `.writeValue()` methods) or returned (by `ObjectMapper` and `ObjectWriter` `createGenerator()` methods).

The main use case is for XML module's initialization of things like XML declaration, but it can be useful for other format backends too.

title: `TypeFactory._fromParamType` `StackOverflowError` with mutually recursive generic bounds (regression in 3.1.0)
body:
### Describe the bug
`TypeFactory._fromParamType` enters infinite recursion when resolving types with mutually recursive generic type parameter bounds. This is a regression introduced in Jackson **3.1.0** — works correctly on **3.0.1**.
### Reproduction
Full reproducer project with failing tests: https://github.com/sylvaindd/jackson-recursive-generics-bug
**Type hierarchy forming a mutual recursion cycle:**
```java
abstract class RuleSetRevisionDTO<RS extends RuleSetDTO<?>, RSR extends RuleSetRuleDTO<?, ?>> {}
abstract class RuleSetDTO<RSRV extends RuleSetRevisionDTO<?, ?>> {}
abstract class RuleSetRuleDTO<RR, RS extends RuleSetRevisionDTO<?, ?>> {}
```
**Any of these trigger `StackOverflowError`:**
```java
JsonMapper mapper = JsonMapper.builder().build();
// Via TypeReference
mapper.readValue(json, new TypeReference<List<RuleSetRevisionDTO<?, ?>>>() {});
// Via constructCollectionType
mapper.readValue(json, mapper.getTypeFactory()
    .constructCollectionType(List.class, RuleSetRevisionDTO.class));
```
### Stack trace
```
tools.jackson.databind.type.TypeFactory._fromParamType(TypeFactory.java:1567)
tools.jackson.databind.type.TypeFactory._fromAny(TypeFactory.java:1278)
tools.jackson.databind.type.TypeFactory._fromParamType(TypeFactory.java:1567)
tools.jackson.databind.type.TypeFactory._fromAny(TypeFactory.java:1278)
... (1024 frames, infinite recursion)
```
### Expected behavior
`TypeFactory` should detect the cyclic type reference and break the recursion, as it did in **3.0.1**.
### Workaround
Using concrete subclasses (with fully resolved type parameters) avoids the recursive bound resolution:
```java
// StackOverflowError
mapper.readValue(json, new TypeReference<List<RuleSetRevisionDTO<?, ?>>>() {});
// Works — concrete class has no wildcard bounds to recurse on
mapper.readValue(json, mapper.getTypeFactory()
    .constructCollectionType(List.class, ConcreteRuleSetRevisionDTO.class));
```
### Version information
| Version | Result |
|---------|--------|
| **3.0.1** | ✅ Works |
| **3.1.0** | ❌ `StackOverflowError` |

title: Jackson 2.21 throws Conflicting property-based creators if both default (0-arg) and multi-arg constructor annotated
StdDelegatingSerializer.isEmpty() passes wrong argument to _findSerializer, causing ClassCastException with Kotlin value classes + NON_EMPTY
body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

Hello team, 

So that's my first bug report here, with the number of years I used your work guys, I wanted at least to say thanks. :p 

So for the bug in itself it seems that in StdDelegatingSerializer.isEmpty() (line 209), _findSerializer(value, ctxt) passes the original unconverted value instead of delegateValue (the converted value). This is inconsistent with serialize() (line 175) which correctly passes delegateValue.

When _delegateSerializer is null (which happens, I am not certain to understand why yet, with Kotlin inline value classes where the Kotlin module registers a BoxConverter whose delegate is never resolved), the _findSerializer fallback is triggered. It finds a serializer for the wrong type (the original type instead of the converted type), then calls isEmpty() on that serializer with the converted value, causing a ClassCastException.

// isEmpty() — line 209 — BUG
ser = _findSerializer(value, ctxt);        // finds serializer for original type (String)
return ser.isEmpty(ctxt, delegateValue);   // passes converted type (MyId) → ClassCastException

// serialize() — line 175 — CORRECT
ser = _findSerializer(delegateValue, ctxt); // finds serializer for converted type (MyId)
ser.serialize(delegateValue, gen, ctxt);    // passes converted type (MyId) → works

The fix seems to be a one-word change on line 209: value → delegateValue.

### Version Information

jackson-databind 3.0.4 and jackson-kotlin-module 3.0.4

### Reproduction

```kotlin
import com.fasterxml.jackson.annotation.JsonInclude
import com.fasterxml.jackson.annotation.JsonValue
import tools.jackson.databind.json.JsonMapper
import tools.jackson.module.kotlin.KotlinModule

@JvmInline
value class MyId(@get:JsonValue val value: String)

data class MyEvent(val id: MyId, val name: String)

fun main() {
    val mapper = JsonMapper.builder()
        .addModule(KotlinModule.Builder().build())
        .changeDefaultPropertyInclusion {
            JsonInclude.Value.construct(
                JsonInclude.Include.NON_EMPTY,
                JsonInclude.Include.NON_EMPTY
            )
        }
        .build()

    // ClassCastException: class MyId cannot be cast to class java.lang.String
    //   at tools.jackson.databind.ser.jdk.StringSerializer.isEmpty(StringSerializer.java:27)
    //   at tools.jackson.databind.ser.std.StdDelegatingSerializer.isEmpty(StdDelegatingSerializer.java:211)
    //   at tools.jackson.databind.ser.BeanPropertyWriter.serializeAsProperty(BeanPropertyWriter.java:592)
    println(mapper.writeValueAsString(MyEvent(MyId("test-123"), "hello")))
}
``` 
What I think is happening: 

1. The Kotlin module registers a StringValueClassBoxConverter for the id property (boxes String → MyId), but _delegateSerializer remains null and _delegateType is Object.
2. NON_EMPTY inclusion triggers BeanPropertyWriter to call StdDelegatingSerializer.isEmpty().
3. convertValue("test-123") correctly boxes to MyId("test-123").
4. Since _delegateSerializer is null, _findSerializer(value, ctxt) is called with the original String value → resolves StringSerializer.
5. StringSerializer.isEmpty(ctxt, MyId("test-123")) casts MyId to String → ClassCastException.
6. Without NON_EMPTY, isEmpty() is never called and serialize() works fine (because it correctly uses _findSerializer(delegateValue, ctxt)).

### Expected behavior

Serialization should succeed and produce:

{"id":"test-123","name":"hello"}

isEmpty() should pass delegateValue to _findSerializer, matching the behavior of serialize().

Note: There may also be a secondary issue where _delegateSerializer is never resolved (stays null, _delegateType is Object). This could be a jackson-module-kotlin issue in how the BoxConverter is registered. However, even if the delegate is null, fixing _findSerializer to use delegateValue would make isEmpty() work correctly via the dynamic lookup path.

### Additional context

_No response_

body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

The JacksonAnnotationIntrospector.java class references com.fasterxml.jackson.annotation.*
The static ANNOTATIONS_TO_INFER_SER references JsonSerializeAs.class from the 2.21 package, but it is no longer included in the pom.

The result is dat during class initialization an application using jackson-databind 3.1.0 also has to explicitly include the old 2.21 com.fasterxml.jackson.annotations package.

Either it should be included as a transitive dependency, the reference should be dropped, or it should be ported to a tools.jackson variant.

See attached PoC file: poc_jackson_readValue.py

**Summary**
A critical deserialization remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability exists in the `readResolve` method within `ObjectMapper.java` in the jackson-databind library version 3.1.0.

- Impact Subscore: 10.0 (HIGH)

title: Wrong path in `InvalidFormatException` for nested fields and `BuilderBasedDeserializer`
body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

The BuilderBasedDeserializer builds the wrong path for nested fields when a InvalidFormatException is thrown.



### Version Information

3.1.0

### Reproduction

test ---> 
https://github.com/dalbrx-nitrobox/jackson-databind/blob/3.x/src/test/java/tools/jackson/databind/deser/builder/BuilderWithNestedFieldsTest.java

fails with 

`expected: <child.field> but was: <field.field>`

Snippet showing the problem when a field value of a child cannot be mapped:

```java
        String json = """
                {
                    "child": {
                        "field": "invalid"
                    }
                }
                """;

        InvalidFormatException invalidFormatException = assertThrowsExactly(
                InvalidFormatException.class,
                () -> objectMapper.readValue(json, Parent.class));

        String fieldPath = fieldName(invalidFormatException.getPath());

       assertEquals("child.field", fieldPath);
```

Parent.class is annotated with

```java
    @JsonDeserialize(
            builder = Parent.ParentBuilder.class
    )
```

Child.class is annotated with

```java
    @JsonDeserialize(
            builder = Child.ChildBuilder.class
    )
```

### Expected behavior

BuilderBasedDeserializer should build the correct path for nested fields

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Usage of @JsonUnwrapped and @JsonCreator ignores values on deserialization
body: Originally posted by **nuf1wa3-1** March 11, 2026 When upgrading from version 2 to 3 we encounter the following problem with @JsonUnwrapped, @JsonCreator and deserializing a JSON that does not contain all attributes specified in the Objects: Having the following classes (for convenience of this post I used inner classes): ``` @Data public class KafkaMessage { private List signalData = new ArrayList<>(); @Data public static class Signal { @JsonUnwrapped private SignalId signalId; private String signalName; private String unit; @JsonCreator() public Signal(@JsonProperty("signalId") SignalId signalId, @JsonProperty("signalName") String signalName, @JsonProperty("unit") String unit) { this.signalId = signalId; this.signalName = signalName; this.unit = unit; } } public static class SignalId { private String signalId; @JsonCreator public SignalId(String signalId) { this.signalId = signalId; } public SignalId() { signalId = "defaultId"; } public void setSignalId(String signalId) { this.signalId = signalId; } @Override public String toString() { return signalId; } } } ``` When using the ObjectMapper to deserialize JSON like in this test: ``` @Test void testDeserializeKafkaMessage_OK() { String json = """ { "signalData": [ { "signalId": "SIGNAL_1-ID", "signalName": "SIGNAL_1", "unit": "unit_1" } ] } """; ObjectMapper mapper = new ObjectMapper(); KafkaMessage msg; try { msg = mapper.readValue(json, KafkaMessage.class); } catch (Exception e) { fail("Deserialization failed: " + e.getMessage()); return; } KafkaMessage.Signal signalData = msg.getSignalData().getFirst(); assertEquals("SIGNAL_1-ID", signalData.getSignalId().toString()); assertEquals("SIGNAL_1", signalData.getSignalName()); assertEquals("unit_1", signalData.getUnit()); } ``` -> This works nicely! ``` @Test void testDeserializeKafkaMessage_FAILING() { String json = """ { "signalData": [ { "signalId": "SIGNAL_1-ID", "signalName": "SIGNAL_1" } ] } """; ObjectMapper mapper = new ObjectMapper(); KafkaMessage msg; try { msg = mapper.readValue(json, KafkaMessage.class); } catch (Exception e) { fail("Deserialization failed: " + e.getMessage()); return; } KafkaMessage.Signal signalData = msg.getSignalData().getFirst(); assertEquals("SIGNAL_1-ID", signalData.getSignalId().toString()); assertEquals("SIGNAL_1", signalData.getSignalName()); assertNull(signalData.getUnit()); } ``` -> This test fails with "_expected: but was: _" **The difference between both tests is that the failing one does not specify the "unit" attribute.** The general thing is that **if and only if not all attributes** for the object which should be deserialized **are given in the JSON**, all attributes of the deserialized object with an **@JsonUnwrapped annotation contain the default value** (set in the default constructor) **instead of the value defined in the JSON**. **Expected:** Even if not all attributes of an object are specified in the JSON which is to be deserialized: The specified attributes are set in the objects created by the ObjectMapper. **Observed:** If not all attributes of an object are specified in the JSON which is to be deserialized: The specified attributes with an @JsonUnwrapped annotation contain the value set in the default constructor instead of the value defined in the JSON. When debugging the code its obvious that for the failing case (not all attributes are given in the JSON) **for all attributes annotated with @JsonUnwrapped first the @JsonCreator annotated constructor is called and afterwards the default constructor is called**. BUT the setSignalId(String signalId) method is not called, therefore leaving the object initialized with the default value. Looking around in the various forums it seems that the combined usage of @JsonUnwrapped and @JonCreator is discouraged. **Question**: Is that the intended behaviour or is that a bug? How to handle this? Her are the complete src files: [jackson-test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25897945/jackson-test.zip)

title: `DecimalNode.equals()` does not compare scale (`10.00` and `10.0000` considered equal)

labels.name: will-not-fix
labels.description: Closed as either non-issue or something not planned to be worked on

labels.name: works-as-intended
labels.description: For issues closed due to (new) behavior observed is as intended and not a flaw

body:
We use a tree representation of json values `JsonNode`. And we want to be able to differentiate between value 10.00 and 10.0000.
Therefore we use the following settings:
JsonNodeFeature.USE_BIG_DECIMAL_FOR_FLOATS (true) and JsonNodeFeature.STRIP_TRAILING_BIGDECIMAL_ZEROES (false)

The node is represented correctly using a DecimalNode. However when comparing both nodes they end up to be equal. This is because equals implementation uses compareTo method instead of equals. compareTo does not compare scale.

title: `@JsonUnwrapped`doesn't work for delegating ValueDeserializer (even one delegating to `BeanDeserializerBase`)
`VisibilityChecker` overrides `equals()` without overriding `hashCode()`
body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

**Labels:** bug

**Body:**

### Summary

`VisibilityChecker` overrides `equals()` with a value-based comparison of 6 visibility level
fields, but does not override `hashCode()`. This violates the `Object` contract (JDK 21 Javadoc):

> "If two objects are equal according to the `equals(Object)` method, then calling the `hashCode`
> method on each of the two objects must produce the same integer result."

Two `VisibilityChecker` instances with identical field values will be `equals()` but may have
different `hashCode()` values.

### Impact

Using `VisibilityChecker` in hash-based collections (`HashSet`, `HashMap`) will produce
incorrect behavior: equal objects may be stored in different buckets.

### How this was found

Detected by an automated JDK conformance oracle (`ObjectOracles.checkEqualsHashCodeOverride`).

title: `TokenBuffer` serialization fails when buffer contains integer encoded as String
body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

`TokenBuffer` serialization does not consider the case when buffer contains integer value encoded as `String`. After it is determined that the value is not `Integer`, `BigInteger`, `Long` or `Short`, serialization logic tries to cast it to `Number` and fails with `ClassCastException`.

I first encountered this issue when trying to serialize a `TokenBuffer` that was previously copied from `JsonParser` which pointed at a big integer value. Copying was done using `copyCurrentStructure()` which called `_copyBufferValue()` which in turn [wrote a big integer](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/blob/61a16f22f6eb132b07e6bf3277f10926919c0a52/src/main/java/tools/jackson/databind/util/TokenBuffer.java#L1336) using a "deferred" value which at that point turned out to be a `String` because it hasn't been decoded yet.

After examining `TokenBuffer` serialization logic I realized that this behaviour was not specific to big integers and it would lead to the same serialization error for any integer encoded as `String`.

### Version Information

First encountered in 2.19.4 but it still exists in 3.x

### Reproduction

Reproducing test case: https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/blob/53baad9bae9f0a64b05dd8cefdb14cd453d66d62/src/test/java/tools/jackson/databind/util/TokenBufferTest.java#L313-L319

body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

I'm migrating a custom JsonSerializer from Jackson 2.x to Jackson 3 and encountered an API change that prevents me from achieving the desired behavior.

In Jackson 2, I could access the ObjectMapper inside serialize via JsonGenerator.getCodec() and then create an ObjectWriter with a specific view:
```java
public void serialize(Object value, JsonGenerator gen, SerializationContext serializers) {
    ObjectMapper mapper = (ObjectMapper) gen.getCodec();
    ObjectWriter writer = mapper.writerWithView(MyView.class);
    // use writer to serialize something with that view
}
```
This pattern was used to temporarily switch the active view for nested objects during serialization, as described in [jackson-annotations issue #132](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-annotations/issues/132). The goal is to serialize a property using a different view than the one currently active for the root object.

However, in Jackson 3, it appears that JsonGenerator no longer has a getCodec() method (or the concept of a codec has been removed/refactored). I cannot find a way to obtain the root ObjectMapper (or its equivalent) from the generator or the serialization context in order to construct an ObjectWriter with a specific view.

Is there a recommended way to achieve this in Jackson 3? If not, would it be possible to add a method like SerializationContext.writerWithView(Class<?> view) or provide access to the ObjectMapper (or a writer factory) from within a serializer?

Thank you for your work on Jackson!

title: DateTimeException is not caught when we deserialize java.time instances

labels.name: 3.x
labels.description: Issues to be only tackled for Jackson 3.x, not 2.x

body:
Example
```
== Java Exception: java.time.DateTimeException: Invalid date 'FEBRUARY 30'
--
  | at java.base/java.time.LocalDate.create(LocalDate.java:461)
  | at java.base/java.time.LocalDate.of(LocalDate.java:273)
  | at java.base/java.time.LocalDateTime.of(LocalDateTime.java:313)
  | at tools.jackson.databind.ext.javatime.deser.LocalDateTimeDeserializer.deserialize(LocalDateTimeDeserializer.java:139)
  | at tools.jackson.databind.ext.javatime.deser.LocalDateTimeDeserializer.deserialize(LocalDateTimeDeserializer.java:37)
  | at tools.jackson.databind.jsontype.impl.AsArrayTypeDeserializer._deserialize(AsArrayTypeDeserializer.java:115)

```

* DateTimeException is a RuntimeException so users are quite likely not to expect that ObjectMapper.readValue can even throw this exception and may not explicitly handle it as a result
* Add a new tools.jackson.databind.ext.javatime.DateTimeParseException (extends DatabindException)
* in tools.jackson.databind.ext.javatime.deser, all code that parses java.time should catch DateTimeException and rethrow a DateTimeParseException, eg LocalDateTime.of, LocalDateTime.parse
* As well as LocalDateTime, there is LocalDate, LocalTime and other classes where we call static `of` or `parse` methods.

body:
I have a REST json endpoint returning wrapped data:

```
{
  "resourceId": XX,
  "type": YY,
  "resourceData": {...}
}
```

I use individual POJOs for different resource types, and I use a single wrapper POJO as the root wrapper (this could also be something like the JSON:API wrapper)
individual typed resource data are annotated with type specific views. No views at all on the root wrapper, the views depend on the specific types. When serializing the root wrapper with writerForView(specificTypeViewA.class), with DEFAULT_VIEW_INCLUSION=false, the root wrapper is entirely filtered out, the resourceData field is not recursively inspected, 
EDIT: working declaration, use `JsonView(Object.Class)` on the wrapper 

### Describe the solution you'd like

Annotate at the class level or field level that even with `Mapper.DEFAULT_VIEW_INCLUSION=false`, these are still included

### Usage example

I haven't given much thought about naming: something like this ?

```java
public class Wrapper<T> {

   @DefaultViewInclusion(ALWAYS)
   T data; // always include independant of the view

   String DontCareOptional; // this one the caller may include or not depending on global config
}
```

or if many fields: 

```java
@DefaultViewInclusion(ALWAYS)
public class Wrapper<T> {

   T dataA; // always include independant of the view
   T dataB; // always include independant of the view
   T dataC; // always include independant of the view
   T dataD; // always include independant of the view

   @DefaultViewInclusion(AUTO)
   String DontCareOptional; // this one the caller may include or not depending on global config
}
```

Bonus (see "additional context")
replace
```java
// replace
@JsonView(NeverUseMe.class) // at class or field level
// with
@DefaultViewInclusion(NEVER) // more explicit, no class convention?
```


### Additional context
EDIT: removed wrong conclusions that it's not possible, it is with   `@JsonView(Object.class)` (except for very rare edge cases around primitive types like int.class)

EDIT: so thinking about this in terms of types and subclassing may be confusing and could be reframed as a separate concept ?

Note: It's already possible to do the the opposite based on convention (annotate at class/field level that even with DEFAULT_VIEW_INCLUSION=true, these are still excluded, with JsonView(NeverUseMe.class). But maybe it's worth it to use same new the declaration system to not even rely on the fact that the caller will not try to do writerWithView(NeverUseMe.class) ?

Confusing error-handling logic in `FunctionalScalarDeserializer` (Wrapped with exceptions)

labels.name: 3.x
labels.description: Issues to be only tackled for Jackson 3.x, not 2.x

body:
Follow up on Issue #4004 and PR https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/pull/5595 ### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe. Long story short, **error types are confusing and sometimes misleading.** Jackson 3.1 introduced `FunctionalScalarDeserializer` that provides a rich and extensible deserialization model, but error handling in user-provided deserializers is currently too loose, leading to loss of semantic meaning in exception types. #### Problematic scenario Consider the following test: ```java @Test public void justThrowErrorEvenWithValidFormat() throws Exception { SimpleModule module = new SimpleModule("test"); module.addDeserializer(Bar.class, new FunctionalScalarDeserializer<>(Bar.class, (p, ctx) -> { // 1) Blindly throw user/business exception throw new IllegalArgumentException( "BiFunction error: " + p.getValueAsString()); })); ObjectMapper mapper = jsonMapperBuilder() .addModule(module) .build(); try { // 2) JSON input is fully valid mapper.readValue("{\"bar\":\"fieldValue\"}", BarWrapper.class); fail(); } catch (InvalidFormatException e) { // 3) Jackson wraps the exception as InvalidFormatException verifyException(e, "Cannot deserialize"); } } ``` In this case: 1. User code throws an arbitrary exception (`IllegalArgumentException`) 2. The JSON input is valid 3. Jackson wraps the failure as `InvalidFormatException` <--- why? From a user perspective, encountering `InvalidFormatException` strongly suggests that the input value itself is invalid or malformed. However, in this scenario the failure is entirely due to application/domain logic, not input format. This makes it difficult for users to correctly interpret and classify errors (e.g. client error vs server error). There could be more cases. ### Describe the solution you'd like **Goal: Make exception wrapping from user-provided deserializers predictable** Jackson should apply deterministic wrapping rules when exceptions are thrown from user-provided deserialization callbacks (for example, `FunctionalScalarDeserializer`), so that the resulting exception type reflects the nature of the failure. --- ### Proposed behavior 1. Provide guidelines on HOW user should throw error (and which type) 2. DO improve Exception handling logic within `FunctionalScalarDeserializer` Below is my rough idea of mentioned guideline. ```java /** * A general-purpose deserializer that uses a {@link Function} or {@link BiFunction} * to convert JSON scalar values (strings, numbers, booleans) into target type instances. *

* This deserializer is primarily designed for String-based conversions but also * supports other JSON scalar types via {@code getValueAsString()} coercion. * Non-scalar JSON values (arrays, objects, embedded objects) are rejected. *

* Error handling guidelines: *

    *
  • Exceptions of type {@link com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidFormatException} * thrown by user code are treated as explicit signals of invalid input values * and are propagated as-is.
    • *
  • {@link com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.JsonMappingException} may be thrown by * user code to indicate a deliberate mapping failure and will be propagated.
    • *
  • Other runtime exceptions thrown by user code are considered application or * domain failures and may be wrapped as {@link com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.JsonMappingException} * rather than being reported as input-format errors.
    • *
*

* Usage examples: * ...... rest omitted..... ``` --- ### Exception handling rules | Thrown by user code | Jackson behavior | |---------------------|------------------| | InvalidFormatException | Propagate as-is | | JsonMappingException | Propagate as-is | | Other Exceptions | Wrap in JsonMappingException | --- ### Resulting Benefit - Valid JSON input is not reported as format error - User intent is preserved when signaling mapping failures - Custom deserializer error behavior becomes consistent and predictable

title: Rule 2 of issue #5318 is not met
body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

### Describe the bug

If a bean has a no-args constructor, and a constructor with some parameters not related to the properties of the bean,
then this second constructor is called with null values (because the parameters are not fields of the JSON).
If this constructor then throws some exception (e.g. NullPointerException because the parameters are null), then the unmarshalling of the JSON to the bean is aborted.

If rule 2 of issue #5318 would be obeyed, constructor 2 would not be eligible, because it does not have parameters for all properties of the bean.

BeanDeserializerBase line 245 sets _nonStandardCreation to true
if _valueInstantiator.canCreateFromObjectWith() returns true,
which is the case if the StdValueInstantiator found any constructor with some parameters,
even if they have nothing to do with the properties of the bean.

### Version Information

jackson-databind 3.0.3

### Reproduction

Example class that cannot be deserialized with jackson-databind 3.0.3 :

```java
  public class Point {
    private int x, y;
  
    public Point() {
    }
  
    public Point(String description) {
      throw new NullPointerException("Should not be called");
    }
  
    ... getters and setters for x and y
  }
``` 

Note: if the Point class would have a third constructor, then everything is fine, because the no-args constructor is called.
This is because of rule 1 of issue #5318 .

### Expected behavior

A constructor with parameters should only be called if all the parameters correspond to properties of the bean.
Then those parameters receive values from the JSON fields.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Improve `asXxx()` method impls of `MissingNode`, `POJONode(null)`, to work like `NullNode`
body:
Currently (3.0.x) coercing non-defaulting accessors like `JsonNode.asString()` fail for `MissingNode` (and `POJONode` constructed to wrap `null`. They should instead work like `NullNode.asString()` -- similarly for other `asXxx()` accessors.

Defaulting (`asString(String defaultValue)`) and optional (`asStringOpt()`) should work as-is, returning default / Optional.empty()` value.

title: Add functional conversion methods `JsonNode.nullAs()`, `JsonNode.missingAs()`
body:
There is need for additional functional transformation methods, and beyond general-purpose `JsonNode.map()` (see #5579) we probably could use some convenience variants too:

* `JsonNode.nullAs(JsonNode)` / `JsonNode.nullAs(Supplier<? extends JsonNode>)` 
* `JsonNode.missingAs(JsonNode)` / `JsonNode.missingAs(Supplier<? extends JsonNode>)` 

in which in first case, converter (`Supplier`) is only called if node is a `NullNode` (`isNull()` returns `true`), and in second case if it is `MissingNode` (`isMissingNode()` returns `true`).

title: Add `JsonNode.map()` method
body:
As a simple functional add-on, let's add something like this on `JsonNode`:

    public <R> R map(Function<? super JsonNode, ? extends R> mapper);
